coredata.dc:title: Hetero atoms doped carbon dots modified electrodes for the sensitive and selective determination of phenolic anti-oxidant in coconut oil
coredata.dc:creator: Karthikeyan, R.
Nelson, D. James
Ajith, Ajay
John, S. Abraham
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) and CDs doped with nitrogen (N-CDs) and nitrogen-sulphur (N,S-CDs) were prepared by ultrasonication using different precursors. The HR-TEM images showed the average particle size of CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs was found to be 1.6, 2.0 and 1.9nm, respectively. The synthesized different CDs were modified on glassy carbon (GC) electrode by immersing it on the respective CDs for 8h. The modification of CDs was confirmed by attenuated total reflectance (ATR)-FT-IR, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques. The ATR-FT-IR spectrum of GC substrate modified with CDs shows characteristic carboxyl stretching at 1723cm−1 whereas NH stretching at 3345cm−1 for N-CDs and 3384cm−1 for N,S-CDs. The heterogeneous rate constant (k et) calculated for 1mM Ru (NH3)6Cl3 at bare GC, GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes was found to be 0.92×10−4, 1.79×10−3, 2.1×10−3 and 3.05×10−3 cms−1, respectively. The higher k et value obtained at GC/N,S-CDs electrode indicates that the electron transfer was faster at this electrode compared to other electrodes. The electrocatalytic activity of GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes towards the oxidation of tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) was studied. It was found that the GC/N,S-CDs electrode showed higher oxidation current than other electrodes. It showed 4-fold higher oxidation current with 177mV less positive potential for TBHQ when compared to bare GC electrode. This was attributed to higher electroactive surface area, hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions between N,S-CDs and TBHQ. Under optimized conditions, N,S-CDs modified electrode exhibited a linear response in a wide concentration range from 50×10−9-10×10−6 M TBHQ and a limit of detection was found to be 0.68ngL−1 (S/N=3). Finally, the present modified electrode was successfully utilized for the determination of TBHQ in coconut oil samples and the results were validated with GC–MS method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
N-doped carbon dots
N,S-doped carbon dots
Tert-butylhydroquinone
Amperometry
Coconut oil

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of different CDs modified electrodes
Characterization of CDs by UV, FT-IR, fluorescence and HR-TEM techniques
Characterization of different CDs modified GC electrodes
Electrochemical behaviour of [Ru(NH3)6]2+/3+ at CDs modified electrodes
Characterization of CDs modified electrode by XRD, XPS and SEM
Electrochemical behaviour of TBHQ at CDs modified electrodes
Determination of TBHQ by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV)
Amperometric determination of TBHQ using GC/N,S-CDs electrode
Stability of the N,S-CDs modified electrode towards the determination of TBHQ

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images obtained for (A) CDs, (B) N-CDs and (C) N,S-CDs. Inset: The corresponding SAED patterns. Average size distribution plot for (a) CDs, (b) N-CDs and (c) N,S-CDs.

(A) FT-IR spectra obtained for (a) glucose, (b) L-methionine, (c) CDs, (d) N-CDs and (e) N,S-CDs. ATR-FT-IR spectra obtained for (B): (a) CDs and (b) GC/CDs; (C): (a) N-CDs and (b) GC/N-CDs and (D) (a) N,S-CDs and (b) GC/N,S-CDs substrates.
CVs obtained for 1mM Ru(NH3)6Cl3 in 0.2M PB solution (pH3) at (a) bare GC, (b) GC/CDs, (c) GC/NCDs and (d) GC/N,S-CDs electrodes at a scan rate of 50mVs−1.
XRD patterns obtained for (a) GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and (c) GC/N,S-CDs substrates.
(a) XPS survey spectrum and (b) XPS of C 1s, (c) O 1s, (d) N 1s and (e) S 2p regions obtained for N,S-CDs modified GC substrate.
CVs obtained for 1mM TBHQ in 0.2M PB solution (pH7.2) at (a) bare GC, (b) GC/CDs, (c) GC/N-CDs and (d) GC/N,S-CDs electrodes at a scan rate of 50mVs−1.
DPVs obtained at (A) GC/CDs, (B) GC/N-CDs and (C) GC/N,S-CDs electrodes for each addition of 20μM TBHQ in 0.2M PB solution (pH7.2).

Amperometric i-t curves obtained for (A) each 50nM addition of TBHQ and (B) addition of (a) 50nM, (b) 100nM, (c) 200nM, (d) 500nM, (e) 1μM, (f) 2μM, (g) 5μM and (h) 10μM of TBHQ at the time interval of 50s at GC/N,S-CDs electrode in 0.2M PB solution (Eapp =+0.50V). Inset B shows the calibration plot.
DPVs obtained at GC/N,S-CDs electrode in the absence (a) and (b) presence of coconut oil and (c–e) addition of each 3.3mg TBHQ in 0.2M PB solution (pH7.2).
Comparison of the performance of the present modified electrode towards the detection of TBHQ with the reported papers.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: GNs-SWCNTs-graphene-single walled carbon nanotube nanocomposite; Ch-choline, pPy-polypyrrole; AuNPs-gold nanoparticles; SPE-screen-printed electrode, MWCNTs-multiwalled carbon nanotubes; CPE-carbon paste electrode; CTAB-cetyltrymethylammonium bromide; SDS-sodium dodecylsulphate; HMDE-hanging mercury-drop electrode.

head.ce:title: Hetero atoms doped carbon dots modified electrodes for the sensitive and selective determination of phenolic anti-oxidant in coconut oil

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute, Gandhigram-624 302, Dindigul, Tamilnadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute, Gandhigram-624 302, Dindigul, Tamilnadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) and CDs doped with nitrogen (N-CDs) and nitrogen-sulphur (N,S-CDs) were prepared by ultrasonication using different precursors. The HR-TEM images showed the average particle size of CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs was found to be 1.6, 2.0 and 1.9nm, respectively. The synthesized different CDs were modified on glassy carbon (GC) electrode by immersing it on the respective CDs for 8h. The modification of CDs was confirmed by attenuated total reflectance (ATR)-FT-IR, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques. The ATR-FT-IR spectrum of GC substrate modified with CDs shows characteristic carboxyl stretching at 1723cm−1 whereas NH stretching at 3345cm−1 for N-CDs and 3384cm−1 for N,S-CDs. The heterogeneous rate constant (k et) calculated for 1mM Ru (NH3)6Cl3 at bare GC, GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes was found to be 0.92×10−4, 1.79×10−3, 2.1×10−3 and 3.05×10−3 cms−1, respectively. The higher k et value obtained at GC/N,S-CDs electrode indicates that the electron transfer was faster at this electrode compared to other electrodes. The electrocatalytic activity of GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes towards the oxidation of tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) was studied. It was found that the GC/N,S-CDs electrode showed higher oxidation current than other electrodes. It showed 4-fold higher oxidation current with 177mV less positive potential for TBHQ when compared to bare GC electrode. This was attributed to higher electroactive surface area, hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions between N,S-CDs and TBHQ. Under optimized conditions, N,S-CDs modified electrode exhibited a linear response in a wide concentration range from 50×10−9-10×10−6 M TBHQ and a limit of detection was found to be 0.68ngL−1 (S/N=3). Finally, the present modified electrode was successfully utilized for the determination of TBHQ in coconut oil samples and the results were validated with GC–MS method.

The nitrogen and sulphur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were synthesized and fabricated on glassy carbon electrode (GCE) without using any linker. The N,S-CDs modified electrode was utilized for the sensitive determination of TBHQ in commercially available coconut oil samples.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) is an approved food grade antioxidant that has been used as an efficient preservative for unsaturated vegetable oils, numerous edible animal fats and meat products [1]. According to food and drug administration (FDA), the total antioxidant content of a food containing the additive should not exceed 0.02%. The acceptable daily intake of TBHQ, according to FDA is 0.7mg/kg/day. The FDA permits TBHQ alone or combination with different synthetic preservatives like butylated hydroxyl anisole (BHA) and butylated hydroxy toluene (BHT) [1]. It has been permitted to use in foods up to a maximum limit of 200mgkg−1 in China, United States, Australia, Brazil, New Zealand and Philippines [2]. However, it is banned within the European Union and Japan because of their potential risks such as liver damage and carcinogenesis [3]. Foods that contain TBHQ include processed fats, canola oil, non-organic packed foods, frozen fish products, soft drinks, lipsticks, hair dye and eye shadows [4]. It can induce phase II detoxification enzymes, as well as glutathione S-transferase and NADPH-quinone oxidoreductase [5]. At very high concentration, it has negative health effects on animals, which include producing precursors to abdomen tumors and DNA damage [6]. Recent studies have shown that TBHQ might lead to the formation of 8-hydroxydeoxyguanosine in calf thymus polymer as a result of the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as superoxide and peroxide [7]. TBHQ affects T cells, that are the part of the body's immune system, to release a collection of proteins that will induce allergies to nuts, milk, eggs, wheat and shell fish [8]. The national institute of health reported that consumption of large excess of TBHQ causes vision disturbance, convulsions and medullary dysfunction [9]. Thus, to ensure human health and food safety within the food industry, it is highly desirable to develop a simple method for its determination.

Colorimetry [10], spectrophotometry [11], liquid chromatography [12], gas chromatography [13], high performance liquid chromatography [14], gas chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectroscopy [15], capillary electrophoresis [16,17], flow injection method coupled with electrochemical method [18,19], photoelectrochemical [20,21], FT-IR [22] and surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [23] techniques have been reported for the determination of TBHQ. Although these methods have been successfully used to determine TBHQ with high sensitivity and selectivity, they are expensive, involve tedious sample preparation, require trained technical personnel and laborious. On the other hand, electrochemical technique is less expensive, simple instrumentation and extremely selective and sensitive. Considerable amount of reports were available in the literature for the electrochemical determination of TBHQ [24–35]. The electrodes modified with enzymes, metal nanoparticles, graphene oxide, MWCNT and their polymer composite have been used for the determination of TBHQ [24–35]. The enzyme based biosensors show high sensitivity but they are expensive and denaturation of enzyme is possible during the immobilization process and storage. Similarly, materials like MWCNT, metal nanoparticles and their polymer composite are expensive and involved in complicated electrode modification procedure. Therefore, it is essential to develop a simple electrode modification procedure with cost effective material for the determination of TBHQ.

Carbon dots (CDs) belonging to the carbon family have received arousing attention in recent years due to their strong photoluminescence properties, ease in preparation, low toxicity and large surface area [36–38]. Owing to these properties, they have been exclusively used in bioimaging [39], catalysis [40], biosensors [41], dye-sensitized solar cells [42] and light emitting devices [43]. Typically, CDs have excellent water solubility due to the presence of carboxylic acid moieties at the surface and are facile to functionalize with various organic, polymeric, inorganic or biological species [37]. CDs doped with hetero atoms such as nitrogen, phosphorus, sulphur and boron have altered the optical and electronic properties of CDs [37]. While scanning the literature, CDs have been extensively exploited for fluorescence sensing applications [36–38,44]. But, only little attention has been paid for their electrochemical sensing applications. Pumera and co-workers studied the electrocatalytic activity of CDs and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) modified electrodes towards the oxidation of AA and UA. They found that CDs modified electrode showed greater electrocatalytic activity towards these analytes compared to GQDs modified electrode [45]. Nguyen et al. reported the electrochemical sensing of a drug, etoposide with high sensitivity and selectivity using CDs modified electrode [46]. Besides CDs, N-doped CDs modified electrodes have also been used for the determination of dopamine [47,48], trinitrotoluene [49,50], hydrogen peroxide and paracetamol [51] and glucose [52]. Although CDs and N-doped CDs modified electrodes have been reported for the determination of several biomolecules, no report is available in the literature for the sensing of TBHQ. Further, in the reported papers, either CDs or N-doped CDs modified electrode alone used for the electrochemical sensing application. The electrochemical sensing performance of CDs with respect to different hetero atoms doped modified electrode has not been studied so far. Thus, the objective of the present study is to synthesize CDs and CDs doped with nitrogen and co-doped with nitrogen and sulphur and then attach them on GC electrode for the sensitive and selective determination of TBHQ. The CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs were synthesized by ultrasonication using glucose, ammonium hydroxide, glucose and l-methionine, respectively as precursors. These CDs were attached on GC electrode directly without using any linker and then studied their electrocatalytic activity towards TBHQ. The order of the electrocatalytic activity was found to be N,S-CDs>N-CDs>CDs. Since N,S-CDs modified electrode showed higher electrocatalytic activity it was chosen for the sensitive and selective determination of TBHQ. The oxidation current of TBHQ at N,S-CDs modified electrode was linearly increased with increasing the concentration from 50×10−9-10×10−6 M and limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 0.68ngL−1 (S/N=3). The present modified electrode was further utilized for the determination of TBHQ in commercially available coconut oil samples. Among the limited number of works on CDs modified electrodes, this is the first report that includes the comparison of the electrocatalytic activity of different hetero atoms doped CDs with the CDs modified electrode.

Glucose, sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O), disodium hydrogen phosphate dihydrate (Na2HPO4·2H2O) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Merck, India and were used as received. Tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. L-methionine was purchased from Hi-media. Glassy carbon plates were purchased from Alfa-Aeaser. Phosphate buffer (PB) of different pH solutions were prepared by mixing NaH2PO4·2H2O and Na2HPO4·2H2O. The pH of the PB solution in the acidic range was adjusted with orthophosphoric acid whereas NaOH was used in the basic pH range. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification. Double distilled water was used for preparing all solutions.

UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded using JASCO V-650 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectral measurements were performed on JASCO FP-8500 spectrofluorimeter equipped with Xenon discharge lamp. The FT-IR measurements were carried out using JASCO FT-IR 4500 equipped with ATR PRO with diamond crystal. HR-TEM images were taken from JEOL JEM 2100, operating at 200kV. X-ray diffraction analysis was carried out with PANaltical XPERT 3 X-ray diffraction unit using Ni-filtered Cu K∝ (λ=1.5406Å). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurements were carried at VEGA3 TESCON, Czech Republic. The gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) measurements were carried out by using Shimadzu GC–MS 2010 ultra-instrument. The electrochemical measurements were carried out with CHI electrochemical workstation (Model 643B, Austin, TX). The conventional two-compartment three electrode cell was used for the electrochemical experiments with mirror polished 3mm glassy carbon (GC) electrode as working electrode. Pt wire as a counter electrode and NaCl saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as a reference electrode. For differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements, a pulse width of 0.06s, amplitude of 0.05V, a sample period of 0.02s and a pulse period of 0.20s were used. All the electrochemical measurements were carried under a nitrogen atmosphere at room temperature.

Carbon dots (CDs) and nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared based on the reported procedures [53,54]. CDs were prepared by one step alkali assisted ultrasonic method using glucose as a precursor [53]. 2g of glucose was dissolved in double distilled water (50mL) to form a clear solution and NaOH (50mL, 1mol/L) was added into this solution. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 4h. The colorless solution turns into dark brown colour indicates the successful formation of CDs. Similarly, N-CDs were prepared by ultrasonication using glucose and ammonium hydroxide as precursors [54]. Nitrogen and sulphur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were prepared from glucose and L-methionine under identical conditions. Briefly, 2g of glucose and 0.5g. of L-methionine were dissolved in 50mL of 1M NaOH. The resultant mixture was sonicated for 2h. The colourless suspension turns into dark brown colour solution, indicating the formation of N,S-CDs. The prepared N,S-CDs were centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 20min. The resulting solution was used for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of different CDs modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for the cleaning of GC electrode is given in the reported paper [55]. The cleaned GC electrode was immersed into the colloidal solution of 50mg/mL of different CDs solution for 8h, followed by rinsed with water. The resulting electrodes were termed as GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes. For SEM, XPS, XRD and ATR-FT-IR measurements, GC plates were used as substrate with similar modification procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs by UV, FT-IR, fluorescence and HR-TEM techniques
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of different CDs was confirmed by UV–visible spectroscopy. Fig. S1A shows the UV–visible spectra obtained for CDs and N-CDs. The precursors, glucose and L-methionine did not show any absorption peak in the UV–visible region (Fig. S1B). The CDs shows an absorption peak at 264nm whereas N-CDs and N,S-CDs show absorption peaks at 272nm and 285nm, respectively due to π-π* transitions similar to polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. In addition to 285nm peak, N,S-CDs exhibit the absorption onset at 340nm due to trapping of excited state energy by surface states [38]. The aqueous solutions of CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs show a strong blue luminescence when exposed to UV light due to the radical recombination of electron-hole pairs [44]. The obtained blue luminescence also confirmed the successful formation of CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs. The introduction of both nitrogen and sulphur atoms on CDs decreased the electron density in the oxygen containing functional groups [44]. Thus, N,S-CDs showed stronger blue luminescence than CDs and N-CDs. Fig. S2 shows the emission spectra obtained for CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs. They showed maximum emission in the range of 440–460nm when excited from 350 to 370nm. When varying the excitation wavelength, the emission maximum also varied. This implies that the prepared CDs were excitation dependent emission behaviour, suggesting that they were not uniform in size. Further, the size of the prepared CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs was analyzed using HR-TEM technique. Fig. 1 shows HR-TEM images obtained for CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs. The TEM images showed that they are quasi-spherical in nature. The average particle size was found to be 1.6, 2.0 and 1.9nm, respectively. The absence of diffraction rings in the selected area electron diffraction patterns indicates that the prepared CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs were in amorphous nature.

Further, the prepared CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs were characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. 2 shows the FT-IR spectra obtained for glucose (curve a), L-methionine (curve b), CDs (curve c), N-CDs (curve d) and N,S-CDs (curve e). The obtained different bands and their assignments are given in Table S1. CDs show stretching vibrations at 3454, 2906, 2826 and 1723cm−1 corresponding to –O-H, CC, CH and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. Asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carbonyl group were observed at 1461 and 1329cm−1, respectively. FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs showed stretching vibrations at 3382, 1629, 1466 and 1349cm−1. A broad band obtained at 3345cm−1 corresponds to both –NH2 and –OH groups. A band at 1629cm−1 was due to the CO stretching vibration of the amide group (curve e). The N,S-CDs show bands at 3384, 2982 and 1673cm−1 correspond to both OH and NH stretching vibrations, CC stretching and CO stretching vibrations of amide group. Asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylic acid group were observed at 1461 and 1349cm−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of different CDs modified GC electrodes
Electrochemical behaviour of [Ru(NH3)6]2+/3+ at CDs modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 3 shows the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) obtained for bare GC, GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs modified electrodes in the presence of 1mM Ru(NH3)6Cl3 in 0.2M PB solution (pH=3) at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. A bare GC electrode shows a reversible redox peak with a peak separation of 74mV corresponding to [Ru(NH3)6]3+/2+ redox couple. In the case of GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes, a reversible redox peak was observed for [Ru(NH3)6]3+/2+ with a peak separation of 72, 70 and 68mV, respectively. In contrast to bare GC electrode, peak separation was decreased and the redox current was increased at CDs modified electrodes. Since the CV measurements were carried out at pH3 PB solution, the carboxylic groups may not be dissociated. Thus, the obtained higher redox current was not due to electrostatic interaction between carboxylate ions and [Ru(NH3)6]3+/2+. Alternatively, the higher redox current for [Ru(NH3)6]3+/2+ was attributed to higher eletroactive surface area of N,S-CDs modified electrode. The electroactive surface area was calculated by using Randles-Sevcik equation (Eq. (1)).
(1)
I p
= 2.69 ×
10 5
n
3 / 2
AD
1 / 2
C
ν
1 / 2
where, ip is maximum current (A), n is number of electrons transferred in redox event (n =1 for [Ru (NH3)6]3+/2+ redox probe), A is the electrochemical surface area (cm2), D is the diffusion coefficient (9.1×10−6 cm2 s−1 for [Ru(NH3)6]3+/2+ redox probe), C is the concentration of the redox probe (1mol/cm3) and ν is the scan rate (50mVs−1). The electroactive surface area calculated from Eq. (1) was found to be 0.057, 0.084, 0.107 and 0.163cm2, respectively for bare GC, GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes. Among the different CDs modified electrodes, GC/N,S-CDs modified electrode showed ~35% higher electroactive surface area than that of bare GC electrode.

Electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is used to investigate the conductive nature of the different CDs modified GC electrodes. Fig. S3 shows the Nyquist plots for bare GC, GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes in 1mM Ru(NH3)6Cl3 in 0.2M PB solution (pH3) at scanning frequencies from 0.01 to 100,000Hz. The obtained Nyquist plots were best fit with Randles equivalent circuit model [Rs(CPE-Rp)] (inset S3). The charge transfer resistance (Rct) can be calculated from the semi-circle obtained in the Nyquist plot. Bare GC electrode shows the Rct value of 40.96kΩ whereas the GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes show 21.10, 18.10 and 12.45kΩ, respectively. The different parameters obtained from impedance measurements are given in Table S2. The less Rct value at GC/N,S-CDs electrode indicates that its conductivity was higher than that of other electrodes. The heterogeneous electron transfer rate constant (k et) can be calculated for bare GC, GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes using the following Eq. (2).
(2)
k et
=
RT
n 2
F 2
AR ct
C 0
where, R is the gas constant, T is the temperature (298K), F is the Faraday constant (96,485 C), A is the area (0.07cm2), Rct is the charge transfer resistance, C is the concentration of redox couple in the bulk solution and n is the number of electrons transferred per molecule (n =1 for [Ru(NH3)6]3+/2+ redox couple). The calculated k et values are 0.92×10−3, 1.79×10−3 2.10×10−3 3.05×10−3 cms−1 for bare GC, GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes, respectively. The higher k et value obtained at GC/N,S-CDs electrode indicates that the electron transfer reaction was faster at this electrode compared to other electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs modified electrode by XRD, XPS and SEM
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the XRD patterns of GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs substrates. The CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs showed a broad peak at 2θ values of 25.66°, 25.58° and 25.43°, respectively corresponding to C (002) plane of amorphous carbon. In addition to C (002) plane, additional peaks were obtained at 43.7°, 53.6° and 79.2° corresponding to C (111), C (022) and C (200) planes of GC substrate. The presence of C (002) plane of amorphous carbon confirmed the successful attachment of different CDs on GC substrate.

Further, the mode of attachment of N,S-CDs on GC electrode was examined by XPS. Fig. 5a shows the survey spectrum of N,S-CDs modified GC plate. It shows four peaks with binding energies of 281, 396.5, 528.5 and 164.5eV assigned to C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p, respectively. Further, C1s region deconvoluted into four peaks with binding energy of 280.1, 281.4, 283.8 and 286.3eV corresponding to CC, C-OH, C=O/C-N and –COO groups, respectively (Fig. 5b). The XPS of N1s region shows the peaks at 396.5, 398.9 and 404eV corresponding to C-N-C, (C)3-N and NH bonds, respectively (Fig. 5c). Fig. 5d shows the O1s region for various oxygen containing functional groups. It shows peaks at 528.2, 529.5 and 531.8eV corresponds to C=O* (aromatic), C-O* (aromatic) and C-O-C/C-OH bonds. The S2p region shows a peak with a binding energy of 164.5eV corresponds to CS linkage (Fig. 5e). The presence of 283.8eV peak confirmed the attachment of N,S-CDs on GC substrate through CN linkage. The N,S-CDs modified GC surface contains amine groups on the surface and these amine groups undergo Michael's addition reaction with the olefinilic carbon groups of GC surface to form CN bond [56].

The size and morphology of the different CDs modified GC surfaces were characterized by SEM. Fig. S4 represents the SEM images obtained for bare GC, GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs modified GC substrates. The GC/CDs substrate shows the quasi-spherical particles with uniform distribution. The average particle size of CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs was found to be 39, 27 and 45nm, respectively. The particle size of CDs was increased after attached on GC substrate. This may be due to aggregation of the CDs. The SEM results also confirmed the successful attachment of CDs on the GC substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviour of TBHQ at CDs modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 shows the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) obtained for 1mM TBHQ at bare GC, GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes in 0.2M PB solution (pH7.2) at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. The bare GC electrode shows the oxidation peak at 0.33V due to the oxidation of TBHQ to tert-butylquinone (TBQ). In the reverse scan, TBQ was reduced to TBHQ at −0.03V. The potential difference between anodic and cathodic peak potentials was found to be 0.36V. In the case of GC/CDs and GC/N-CDs electrodes, the oxidation peaks were observed at 0.12V and 0.16V, respectively and reduction peaks were obtained at −0.13V and −0.15V, respectively with potential difference of 0.25V and 0.31V. On the other hand, GC/N,S-CDs electrode shows a oxidation peak at 0.15V and a reduction peak at −0.16V with a peak separation of 0.31V. In contrast to GC electrode, the potential difference between the oxidation and reduction peaks of TBHQ at CDs modified electrodes was less, indicating that the reaction was faster at the later electrodes than the former electrode. Among the different electrodes, N,S-CDs modified electrode showed higher oxidation current for TBHQ. It showed 4-fold higher oxidation current and 180mV less positive potential for the oxidation of TBHQ when compared to bare GC electrode. This may be due to the following reasons: (i) large electroactive surface area and high conductance (ii) hydrogen bonding between –OH of TBHQ and –OH, –COOH, N, S groups of N,S-CDs and (iii) π-π interactions between TBHQ and N,S-CDs.

The effect of scan rate on the oxidation of TBHQ at GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs, GC/N,S-CDs electrode was investigated by running CVs at different scan rates from 10 to 100mVs−1 for 1mM TBHQ in 0.2M PB solution (pH7.2) (Fig. S5). While increasing the scan rate from 10 to 100mVs−1, the oxidation peak current of TBHQ was increased with correlation coefficients of 0.9996, 0.9938 and 0.9992 (Fig. S6) indicating that the oxidation of TBHQ at GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes was diffusion controlled process.

Fig. S7 shows the CVs obtained at GC/N,S-CDs electrode for 1mM TBHQ in 0.2M PB solution (pH7.2) at a scan rate of 50mVs−1 at different immersion time ranging from 2 to 10h. When the immersion time increases the oxidation current of TBHQ was increased up to 8h and no significant change in the oxidation current was observed beyond 8h. Hence, 8h was chosen as optimum immersion time for the attachment on GC electrode.

Fig. S8 shows the CVs obtained at GC/N,S-CDs electrode for 1mM TBHQ in the pH ranging from 3 to 11 at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. The oxidation and reduction potentials of TBHQ were shifted towards less positive potentials when pH was increased, indicating that the redox process was influenced by hydrogen ion [54]. While increasing the pH from 3 to 7, the oxidation current of TBHQ was increased and it was decreased when the pH was increased from 7 to 11. As shown in Fig. S8, the higher oxidation current for TBHQ at GC/N,S-CDs electrode was obtained at pH7. This may be due to the proton groups of TBHQ contributes to weakening of the hydroxyl bond energies to facilitate electron transfer through O–H⋯N [54]. The plot of oxidation potential against pH is shown in Fig. S8 (inset). It shows that the potential was shifted to less positive potential while increasing pH with a linear relationship Ep=−0.061 pH+0.70 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9923 and a slope of 61mV/pH. This reveals that the electrochemical oxidation of TBHQ at GC/N,S-CDs electrode involves equal number of protons and electrons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TBHQ by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows the DPVs obtained at GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes for each increment of 20μM TBHQ in 0.2M PB solution (pH7.2). The GC/CDs electrode shows the oxidation peak at 0.13V for the addition of 20μM TBHQ in 0.2M PB solution (pH7.2). Further addition of 20μM TBHQ to the same solution, the oxidation current of TBHQ was increased with increasing the concentration in the range of 20 to 300μM without shift in its oxidation potential with a correlation coefficient of 0.9993. In the case of GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes, the oxidation peak of TBHQ was obtained at 0.14 and 0.12V, respectively for each addition of 20μM TBHQ in 0.2M PB solution (pH7.2). The oxidation peak current of TBHQ was linearly increased with the increasing concentration in the range of 20 to 300μM on further each addition of 20μM TBHQ to the same solution. A linear relationship between the oxidation current of TBHQ and concentration of TBHQ with correlation coefficients of 0.9995 and 0.9993, respectively was obtained (Fig. S9). Further, the sensitivity of GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes was calculated from the slope of the linear calibration curve. The GC/CDs, GC/N-CDs and GC/N,S-CDs electrodes show the sensitivity of 0.020, 0.025 and 0.027μAμM−1, respectively. Among the different electrodes, GC/N,S-CDs electrode showed higher sensitivity towards the oxidation of TBHQ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amperometric determination of TBHQ using GC/N,S-CDs electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Since GC/N,S-CDs electrode showed higher sensitivity towards TBHQ than other electrodes, it's sensitive determination was examined by amperometry. Fig. 8A and B show the amperometric i-t curve obtained for TBHQ at GC/N,S-CDs electrode in a homogeneously stirred 0.2M PB solution (pH7.2) by applying the constant potential of +0.50V. The GC/N,S-CDs electrode shows the initial current response due to the addition of 50nM TBHQ. Further addition of 50nM TBHQ in each step with a sample interval of 50s increases the current response of TBHQ and steady state current response was reached within 3s. The amperometric current response of TBHQ was linearly increased from 50 to 650nM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9994. Fig. 8B shows the amperometric current response increased while increasing TBHQ concentration in the dynamic range of 50nM to 10μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9992 and the limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 0.68ngL−1 (S/N=3). The obtained linear range and LOD of TBHQ at GC/N,S-CDs electrode were compared with the reported modified electrodes (Table 1 ) [24–34]. It is evident from Table 1 that the present GC/N,S-CDs electrode has several advantages which include simple electrode modification procedure, low cost besides high detection limit with wide range determination of TBHQ.

Fig. S10 shows the amperometric i-t curve for TBHQ at GC/N,S-CDs electrode in the presence of several interferences in a homogeneously stirred 0.2M PB solution (pH7.2). The increased initial current response was due to the addition of 100nM TBHQ (curve a) and no change in the current response was observed with the addition of 100μM interferents including MgSO4, CaCl2, NaCl, K2CO3, NaF, NH4Cl, Na2HPO4 urea, glucose, oxalic acid, (curve b-k) and 100μM butylhydroxyanisole (BHA), butylhydroxytoluene (BHT) and propylgallate (PG) (curves l-n). Interestingly, the GC/N,S-CDs electrode showed high selectivity towards TBHQ even in the presence of major interferences such as BHA, BHT and PG. The above results indicate that selective determination of TBHQ is possible even in the presence of 1000-fold excess of common interferences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the N,S-CDs modified electrode towards the determination of TBHQ
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the stability of the GC/N,S-CDs modified electrode, the DPVs were recorded for 0.5mM TBHQ in 0.2M PB solution (pH7.2). It was found that the oxidation current of TBHQ was unchanged with a relative standard deviation of 1.5 and 1.8%, respectively for 25 repetitive measurements, indicating that the modified electrode has a good reproducibility. The oxidation current of TBHQ was decreased by about 0.5% in 4weeks when the electrode was kept in PB solution (pH7.2). To ascertain the reproducibility of the results further, five different N,S-CDs modified electrodes and their response towards the oxidation of 0.5mM TBHQ was recorded by 10 repeated measurements. The peak current showed a relative standard deviation of 1.5%, confirming that the results are reproducible. The above results revealed that the N,S-CDs modified electrode was very stable and reproducible for the determination of TBHQ.

The practical application of the GC/N,S-CDs modified electrode was examined by determining the concentration of TBHQ in coconut oil sample. The coconut oil samples were purchased from the local departmental store. They were dissolved in ethanol and centrifuged at 3000rpm for 10min. Fig. 9 shows the DPV obtained at GC/N,S-CDs electrode in 0.2M PB solution (pH7.2). It shows no electrochemical response in 0.2M PB solution. However, addition of 100μL of aliquot coconut oil in 0.2M PB solution (pH7.2) shows an oxidation peak at 0.16V which may be due to the oxidation of TBHQ. To confirm this, a known concentration of commercial TBHQ (3.3mg) was added to the same solution. The oxidation current at 0.16V was enhanced (curve c) indicating that the oxidation peak is corresponding to the oxidation of TBHQ. Further addition of coconut oil samples shows increase in the oxidation current of TBHQ and good recovery results were obtained (Table S3). The obtained results were validated with GC–MS (Fig. S11). The values evaluated from the present method fairly match with the GC–MS method. These results strongly suggest that the GC/N,S-CDs electrode is highly suitable for the determination of TBHQ in coconut oil samples. Thus, the present modified electrode is very useful to monitor the level of TBHQ in real samples to ensure the human health and food safety.

In this paper, sensitive and selective determination of TBHQ using N,S-CDs modified electrode was discussed. The CDs and hetero atoms doped CDs were synthesized by ultrasonication method using different precursors. The formation of CDs, N-CDs and N,S-CDs was confirmed by UV–vis, spectrofluorimetry and TEM. The CDs were then attached on GC electrode directly without using any linker and studied their electrocatalytic activity towards TBHQ. Among the different electrode, N,S-CDs modified electrode showed higher oxidation current for TBHQ. This was attributed to large surface area, presence of hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions between TBHQ and N,S-CDs. The amperometric current response of TBHQ at N,S-CDs modified electrode was linearly increased with increasing TBHQ concentration in the range of 50×10−9–10×10−6 M and LOD was found to be 0.68ngL−1 (S/N=3). Further, the selective determination of TBHQ even in the presence of 1000-folds higher concentrations of the major interferents BHA, BHT and PG besides common interferents was achieved. Finally, the practical application of the present modified electrode was successfully demonstrated by determining TBHQ in coconut oil samples and the results were validated with GC–MS method.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: R. Karthikeyan thanks the University Grants Commission (UGC), New Delhi for the financial support under the scheme of meritorious student fellowship [F. No. 25-1/2014-15 (BSR)/7-225/2008 (BSR)]. Financial support from Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), Department of Science and Technology (EMR/2016/002898), New Delhi is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary data containing UV-visible, emission, SEM, CV, impedance and amperometry for the CDs are available.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2019.113297.
